MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper includes a basic unigram baseline and evaluates SVMs with feature ablations (unigram, Location, Textual, Language), and uses the same data and metrics (unweighted average F) for fair internal comparisons. However, from the summary it appears to lack stronger and more recent baselines (e.g., neural classifiers or sequence models) and no statistical significance testing or multiple baseline variants beyond unigram are reported — a shortcoming given my expectation for robust baselines and ablations. The authors do cover related work adequately, but they do not appear to acknowledge or compare to prior methods that might perform comparably, so comparisons are incomplete. | B(3): The paper includes basic comparisons (an unigram baseline and SVMs with several feature groups) and evaluates on the same corpus with appropriate metrics, so the experimental conditions are fair. However, from the summary the evaluation omits stronger or more recent baselines (e.g., neural classifiers or richer sequence/structure-aware models) and no hyperparameter/code details are reported, limiting reproducibility and depth of comparison. The authors do acknowledge limitations and cover related work, but the empirical comparisons are modest for the claim of enabling automatic classification.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper includes a basic unigram baseline and evaluates SVMs with feature ablations (unigram, Location, Textual, Language), and uses the same data and metrics (unweighted average F) for fair internal comparisons. However, from the summary it appears to lack stronger and more recent baselines (e.g., neural classifiers or sequence models) and no statistical significance testing or multiple baseline variants beyond unigram are reported — a shortcoming given my expectation for robust baselines and ablations. The authors do cover related work adequately, but they do not appear to acknowledge or compare to prior methods that might perform comparably, so comparisons are incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper includes basic comparisons (an unigram baseline and SVMs with several feature groups) and evaluates on the same corpus with appropriate metrics, so the experimental conditions are fair. However, from the summary the evaluation omits stronger or more recent baselines (e.g., neural classifiers or richer sequence/structure-aware models) and no hyperparameter/code details are reported, limiting reproducibility and depth of comparison. The authors do acknowledge limitations and cover related work, but the empirical comparisons are modest for the claim of enabling automatic classification.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (new corpus, two-tier schema, and classifier experiments). Notation and annotation scheme are presented consistently and inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.84 high-level, 0.71 low-level) and evaluation metrics (unweighted F, SVM feature groups) are reported, and figures/tables/examples appear informative. Minor presentation gaps remain: the paper could more explicitly map the adapted two-tier schema to existing theoretical frameworks and provide fuller reproducibility details (code/hyperparameters), which would improve clarity for readers replicating the work. | B(4): The paper is well-organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early — the new corpus, annotation schema, and two applications — which makes it easy to follow from a resource-release perspective. Writing quality is reported as good and annotation agreement and evaluation metrics are presented, and figures/tables/examples are described as effective; however the summary omits details like hyperparameters and code release, and notation/details for some methodological choices could be expanded, so a small number of presentation clarifications would improve reproducibility and clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (new corpus, two-tier schema, and classifier experiments). Notation and annotation scheme are presented consistently and inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.84 high-level, 0.71 low-level) and evaluation metrics (unweighted F, SVM feature groups) are reported, and figures/tables/examples appear informative. Minor presentation gaps remain: the paper could more explicitly map the adapted two-tier schema to existing theoretical frameworks and provide fuller reproducibility details (code/hyperparameters), which would improve clarity for readers replicating the work.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early — the new corpus, annotation schema, and two applications — which makes it easy to follow from a resource-release perspective. Writing quality is reported as good and annotation agreement and evaluation metrics are presented, and figures/tables/examples are described as effective; however the summary omits details like hyperparameters and code release, and notation/details for some methodological choices could be expanded, so a small number of presentation clarifications would improve reproducibility and clarity.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a substantive contribution by providing a publicly released three-draft argumentative revision corpus with strong inter-annotator agreement and reasonable discussion of related work and limitations. Major weaknesses are limited theoretical mapping, underspecified dataset details, and weakened reproducibility and experimental depth (no released code, limited baselines/ablation), but these do not outweigh the clear empirical value of the resource; therefore a score of 4 reflects solid substance with room for stronger reproducibility and deeper analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a solid annotated corpus with strong IAA reported separately for tiers (κ=0.84 high-level, 0.71 low-level) and reasonable related-work and limitations discussion, which supports the main claims at a descriptive level. However, from the summary I see no explicit formal/theoretical mapping of the adapted two-tier schema to existing argumentation or discourse theory (penalized), dataset size/diversity is unspecified (raises concerns about generality), and reproducibility is weak (no code released, hyperparameters not reported, sentence-alignment/correction procedures underspecified). Empirically the modeling compares mainly to a unigram baseline and lacks broader baselines, thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses, and statistical testing, so experimental depth is adequate but not comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides substantive empirical support for its claims: a publicly released three-draft argumentative revision corpus with sentence alignments and manual purpose annotations (high-level κ=0.84, low-level κ=0.71) and a demonstration of automatic classification using SVMs with several feature groups against a unigram baseline. Related work is reported as adequate and the authors explicitly discuss limitations (population, coarse-grained schema, lack of direct essay-quality labels). However, reproducibility is weakened by no mention of released code or hyperparameters, and the experimental analysis could use more ablation/sensitivity studies (e.g., per-feature-group contributions and classifier tuning) to fully characterize robustness.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the core contribution—a publicly released, manually annotated three-draft, sentence-aligned corpus with solid inter-annotator agreement—is supported, and that classifier experiments are reasonable. Remaining concerns are largely about documentation and reproducibility (formal grounding of the schema, more detail on alignment procedures, dataset statistics, hyperparameters, significance testing, and code release) rather than fundamental flaws in soundness, so a slightly above-average score reflects a technically solid paper that needs better methodological reporting.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are generally supported: it releases a three-draft corpus with sentence alignments and manually coded revision purposes, and reports reasonable annotation reliability (Cohen's κ = 0.84 high-level, 0.71 low-level). However, from a theory-first and reproducibility standpoint the work falls short: the adapted two-tier schema lacks explicit formal grounding or mapping to existing argumentation frameworks, sentence-alignment/correction procedures are underspecified, and dataset size/diversity are not quantified. Experimental methodology is also limited — only a unigram baseline plus SVM variants are presented, hyperparameters and statistical significance testing are not reported — which weakens claims about classifier performance. Overall the technical substance is solid but several methodological and reporting gaps prevent a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims (a publicly released, manually corrected sentence-aligned corpus with three drafts and a two-tier revision schema) are supported by concrete annotation procedures and agreement scores (κ=0.84 high-level, κ=0.71 low-level). The experimental methodology for automatic classification is reasonable (SVMs, unigram baseline, feature groups, cross-validation, and unweighted average F-score), although the summary omits hyperparameter reporting, statistical significance testing, and explicit code release which would strengthen reproducibility. No mathematical proofs were required, and there are no evident logical gaps in the presented summary, so only minor methodological/documentation issues remain.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper delivers a useful, publicly released corpus with solid annotation reliability (κ=0.84/0.71) and clear applications for argument mining and feedback research. Limitations—restricted population (college essays), coarse categories, and relatively weak modeling baselines—reduce theoretical novelty and generality but do not negate the resource’s practical value and likelihood of broad reuse, so a score of 4 reflects strong practical impact with room for further work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a useful publicly released corpus of three-draft argumentative essays with sentence alignments and an adapted two-tier revision schema, which is a practical resource that can enable work in revision analysis and argument mining. However, from a theory-first perspective the contribution is moderate: the schema is described as adapted but no explicit mapping to prior formal theories is reported in the summary, the dataset size and population diversity are not quantified (raising concerns about generality), and the modelling work evaluates only against a unigram baseline rather than multiple strong baselines or ablations. Given the solid inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.84 high-level, 0.71 low-level) the resource is reliable and will be built upon, but these gaps limit its transformative potential.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important practical gap by releasing a publicly available, richly annotated corpus of multi-draft argumentative essays with sentence-level alignments and a two-tier revision schema—resources that are directly useful for argument mining and automated feedback research. The demonstrated applications (analysis of student revision behavior and automatic revision-purpose classification) show feasibility and immediate reuse; solid annotation agreement (κ=0.84/0.71) and three-draft coverage increase its utility. While the focus on college essays and the current coarseness of categories limit generality, the corpus and baseline experiments are likely to be widely built upon and open clear avenues for follow-up work (finer-grained labels, sub-sentential scope, strength evaluation). Overall, this is a strong, practically impactful resource for the community.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper fills an important empirical gap with a high-quality annotated corpus (strong inter-annotator agreement and useful two-tier schema) and is well aligned with community needs in argument-mining and educational NLP. While Reviewer A correctly notes some limitations (unspecified dataset size/population, limited modelling baselines, and no explicit code/hyperparameter release), these are implementation/details that do not outweigh the clear resource contribution and reproducibility potential; therefore the paper is an excellent fit for the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is squarely within the scope of top-tier NLP venues: it presents a new corpus for revision/argument mining, uses standard annotation and classification practices, and reports solid inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.84 high-level, κ=0.71 low-level). However, from a theory-first evaluation I note gaps that reduce its perfect fit: dataset size and population diversity are not specified, the modeling experiments rely on a single (unigram) baseline and SVM variants without extensive baselines or ablation studies, and code/hyperparameter release is not mentioned. Given strong annotation reliability and clear relevance to the community but limited modelling rigor and unspecified dataset scale, I judge it a good fit with minor scope concerns.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP venue: it releases a new, publicly available argumentative revision corpus with sentence-level alignments and a two-tier annotation schema (Surface vs. Content, eight fine-grained labels), which fills a clear empirical gap for argument-mining and educational NLP research. The task formulation, annotation quality (κ=0.84 high-level, 0.71 low-level), and empirical demonstration (SVM baselines with standard F-score evaluation) use methods and evaluation standards expected by the community. While some limitations (domain of college essays, no essay-quality labels) are acknowledged, the resource orientation, reproducibility potential, and demonstrated applications make it highly relevant to the venue’s audience.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the main contribution is a valuable, publicly released three-draft argumentative revision corpus with sentence-level alignments and a useful two-tier annotation schema, and that the experiments use standard modeling baselines. Given the clear empirical utility, high inter-annotator agreement, and demonstrated applications that will inform downstream work, the contribution merits a positive originality score even though it is primarily a dataset/resource paper rather than a novel algorithmic or theoretical advance.

reviewer_a.justification: The work provides a useful new resource (three-draft argumentative revision corpus with sentence-level alignments and an adapted two-tier schema) and demonstrates applications, which is a meaningful empirical contribution but not a radical methodological advance. From a theory-first perspective the paper appears incremental: the schema is an adaptation rather than a formally justified new annotation framework, and the modeling experiments use standard SVM/unigram baselines with limited methodological novelty. The clear reporting of inter-annotator agreement is a strength, but the contribution primarily expands data availability rather than introducing a fundamentally new idea or perspective that would surprise an expert.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper contributes a clearly new empirical resource—a publicly released corpus of three-draft argumentative essays with sentence-level alignments and a two-tier (Surface vs. Content) schema with eight fine-grained categories—which fills a recognized gap for revision and argument-mining research. While the modeling (SVMs with standard feature groups) is not novel, the annotation scheme, manual correction of alignments, reported agreement (κ=0.84 high-level, 0.71 low-level), and demonstrated applications (behavioral analysis and classifier baselines) differentiate it from prior corpora and would inform experts building downstream tools. Overall this is a notable dataset contribution rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic method.

main_claims: We present a new publicly released corpus of annotated revisions for argumentative essays consisting of three drafts per essay, sentence-level alignments, and manually coded revision purposes.
The corpus uses an adapted two-tier revision schema (Surface vs. Content-based) with eight low-level categories related to argument mining and discourse analysis.
We demonstrate two applications of the corpus: analysis of student revision behavior and training/evaluation of automatic revision-purpose classifiers.
_summary.methodology: Essays (three drafts per student) were sentence-aligned automatically and then manually corrected; sentence-level revisions were annotated by trained annotators into Surface vs. Content-based categories and eight finer categories. For automatic classification, SVM classifiers were trained using unigram, Location, Textual, and Language feature groups and evaluated with cross-validation.
datasets: Argumentative Revision Corpus (three drafts per essay; college student essays)
metrics: Cohen's Kappa (reported for annotation agreement: 0.84 for high-level, 0.71 for low-level)
limitations: No evaluative data on essay quality/improvement is available; degree of improvement is approximated by number of revisions.
Current corpus focuses on college student essays (native and L2), limiting generality to other domains or populations.
Planned augmentations noted (more fine-grained categories, sub-sentential scopes, statement strength), indicating current schema/coarseness as a limitation.

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Tenured NLP researcher with a long track record of work on formal models of discourse and argumentation; many publications on theoretical frameworks and evaluation methodologies for annotation schemes.
traits: Penalises resource papers that lack formal theoretical justification for their annotation schema (will deduct points if no explicit mapping to existing theory or formal definition is provided)

Requires explicit inter-annotator agreement statistics (Cohen's kappa, Krippendorff's alpha) reported for each annotation tier and will downgrade if per-category agreement < 0.6 or if only aggregate numbers are given
Demands strong baselines and ablation studies for modelling experiments (will penalise if fewer than three clear baseline variants or no statistical significance testing is reported)
Skeptical of small or narrowly sourced datasets (will lower score if dataset size < few thousand annotated instances or if population/sample diversity is not quantified)
Prefers precise operational definitions and reproducible alignment procedures (will penalise if sentence-alignment or correction procedures are underspecified or not released)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I focus on theoretical grounding, reliability, and rigorous evaluation; I downgrade empirical artifacts that lack formal justification, clear agreement metrics, or robust baselines.

reviewer_b.name: Resource-and-Application Advocate
reviewer_b.background: Senior applied NLP researcher specializing in datasets and downstream applications (argument mining, educational NLP), with experience releasing widely adopted corpora and benchmarks.
traits: Rewards high-quality, publicly released datasets that fill a clear empirical gap (will raise score if code, data, and annotation guidelines are publicly available)
Values practical annotation schemes that enable downstream tasks and will upscore innovations that bridge surface/content distinctions useful for modeling
Favors empirical demonstration of utility (will positively value even modest classification baselines if they show feasibility for common tasks)
Pragmatic about inter-annotator agreement (accepts moderate agreement if accompanied by clear annotation guidelines and adjudication procedures)
Prioritises reproducibility and documentation (will upscore when alignment corrections, annotation provenance, and feature sets are documented and shared)
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I evaluate resources by potential impact and usability for the community; I reward clear, well-documented corpora and pragmatic demonstrations of their utility even if the modeling is not state-of-the-art.
